unit.title: Willkommen!
unit.sub: Your first words in German — greetings, goodbyes, and your very first conversation.

steps.title: Saying Hello 👋
steps.desc: German greetings change with the time of day — and region! Bavaria greets differently from Berlin.

steps.note: Universal and safe — works in ANY situation. Germans use it just like English 'hello'.
Used until about 10-11 AM. Germans are VERY time-specific with greetings — don't say this at noon!

steps.example: Guten Tag! Kann ich Ihnen helfen?
steps.exampleEn: Good day! Can I help you?
steps.note: The 'safe' formal greeting, roughly 10 AM to 6 PM. If in doubt, 'Guten Tag' is always correct.
From about 6 PM. 'Zusammen' = together — adding it makes it warmer, like 'Good evening, all!'

steps.title: 🧠 See the pattern?
steps.text:
Guten = Good (accusative case — yes, even greetings have grammar!)

Guten Morgen = Good morning
Guten Tag = Good day
Guten Abend = Good evening

All three start with 'Guten' because Morgen, Tag, and Abend are masculine nouns. Your first taste of German grammar! 🇩🇪

steps.q: Which greeting is safe ANY time?

steps.note: Very casual — use with friends and people your age. 'Alles klar?' (All clear?) is the casual 'How are you?'
Used in Hamburg, Bremen, and all of Northern Germany — ANY time of day, not just morning! 'Moin moin' = extra friendly. Say this in the north and you'll get instant respect.
Literally 'Greet God' — the standard greeting in Bavaria, Austria, and parts of Switzerland. NOT religious — just cultural! Say this in Munich and you'll blend right in.
From Latin 'servus' (servant). Used in Bavaria and Austria for BOTH hello AND goodbye — like Dutch 'dag'. Very warm and friendly.

steps.title: 🗺️ Germany is NOT one country!
steps.text:
Germany has STRONG regional identities:

🌊 NORTH (Hamburg, Bremen):
'Moin!' — any time of day
Calm, understated, dry humor

🏔️ SOUTH (Bavaria, Austria):
'Grüß Gott!' / 'Servus!'
Warm, traditional, proud

🏙️ EVERYWHERE:
'Hallo!' / 'Guten Tag!'
Always safe, always correct

Germans notice which greeting you use — it signals where you belong!

steps.q: You're in Hamburg. The LOCAL greeting is:

steps.note: 'Guten' = good (accusative masculine). All time-of-day greetings follow this pattern.
'Abend' is masculine — hence 'Guten' (not 'Gute'). Used from about 6 PM.

steps.title: Saying Goodbye ✌️
steps.desc: German has goodbyes ranging from ultra-formal to adorably casual — and the famous word you can barely pronounce.

steps.note: THE everyday goodbye. The 'Tsch' sound is uniquely German — like 'ch' in 'cheese' but softer. Practice it! Everyone understands 'Tschüss'.
Literally 'Upon seeing again'. Very formal — used with strangers, officials, in shops. 'Auf Wiederhören' = goodbye ON THE PHONE (upon hearing again!).
The adorable diminutive of Tschüss. Adding '-i' makes it cute — very common among friends and young people.

steps.title: 💡 'Bis' = 'Until / See you...'
steps.text:
German builds farewells with 'bis' (until):

Bis morgen! = See you tomorrow!
Bis später! = See you later!
Bis bald! = See you soon!
Bis dann! = See you then!
Bis Montag! = See you Monday!

Just like Dutch 'tot'! Same pattern, different word.

steps.note: 'Bis' + any time = see you at that time. Very productive pattern!

steps.exampleEn: I have to go. See you later!
steps.note: 'Später' = later. 'Ich muss los' = I must go — THE German way to excuse yourself.

steps.example: Bis bald! Pass auf dich auf!
steps.note: 'Bald' = soon. Yes, it looks like English 'bald' but means 'soon'! 'Pass auf dich auf' = take care of yourself.
Notice: 'Gute' (not 'Guten') because 'Nacht' is FEMININE! First gender difference you'll notice. 'Schlaf gut!' = Sleep well!

steps.q: Why is it 'Gute Nacht' but 'Guten Tag'?
opts: Nacht is feminine, Tag is masculine
steps.ans: Nacht is feminine, Tag is masculine

steps.note: 'Tschüss' is casual, 'Auf Wiedersehen' is formal — both correct here.
'Gute' (not Guten) because Nacht is feminine. Your first German gender lesson!

steps.title: Bitte & Danke 🙏
steps.desc: Politeness in German is a BIG deal — and 'bitte' might be the most versatile word in the language.
goals: du vs Sie — THE German distinction

steps.note: Simple and universal. 'Danke schön' = thank you very much. 'Danke sehr' = thank you very much (slightly more formal).

steps.example: Danke schön, das ist sehr nett!
steps.exampleEn: Thank you very much, that's very kind!
steps.note: 'Schön' literally means 'beautiful/nice'. So 'danke schön' = 'thank you beautifully'. Germans appreciate polite phrasing!
More formal/emphatic. 'Viel' = much/many. 'Vielen herzlichen Dank' = many heartfelt thanks (very warm).

steps.title: 🔑 du vs Sie — THE German divide
steps.text:
German has TWO 'you' — and getting it wrong is a BIG social signal:

👤 du (informal): friends, family, children, students, colleagues (often)
→ 'Danke, kannst du mir helfen?'

👔 Sie (formal): strangers, elders, officials, in shops
→ 'Danke, können Sie mir helfen?'

Sie is ALWAYS capitalized (even mid-sentence)!

⚠️ Using 'du' too early = rude
⚠️ Using 'Sie' with friends = cold/weird

When in doubt, use Sie. Germans will say 'Wir können uns duzen' (We can use du) when they're ready.

steps.en: please / you're welcome / here you go
steps.note: THE Swiss army knife of German! Means 'please', 'you're welcome', AND 'here you go'. Also 'bitte?' = 'pardon?' / 'what did you say?'. You'll say 'bitte' 50 times a day.
The polite response to 'danke'. Waiter hands you food: 'Bitte schön!' You thank them: 'Danke schön!' Beautiful exchange.

steps.exampleEn: Excuse me, where is the train station?
steps.note: Long word, essential meaning! Use it to get attention, apologize, or ask for directions. 'Schuld' = fault → 'Ent-schuld-igung' = un-fault-ing.

steps.example: Es tut mir leid, das wusste ich nicht.
steps.exampleEn: I'm sorry, I didn't know that.
steps.note: For genuine apologies (deeper than Entschuldigung). Literally: 'It does me sorrow.' 'Leid' = sorrow/suffering.

steps.q: A waiter brings your food. They say:
You bump into someone. You say:
Your boss is 55. You address them with:

steps.note: Multiple ways to say thanks in German — 'Danke schön' is the most common.
Used for getting attention AND for light apologies. For deeper apology, use 'Es tut mir leid'.

steps.title: What's Your Name? 🤝
steps.desc: Introduce yourself in German — and learn why Germans shake hands SO much.

steps.exampleEn: My name is Thomas. And you?
steps.note: 'Heißen' = to be called. Literally 'I am called...' — Germans don't usually say 'My name is' directly. 'Ich heiße' is THE standard.
Use with people your age, children, in casual settings. 'Wie' = how, 'heißt' = are called, 'du' = you (informal).
The formal version — 'Sie' (always capitalized!) + formal verb conjugation. Use with strangers, elders, in professional settings.

steps.example: Ich bin Lisa. Ich komme aus Berlin.
steps.exampleEn: I am Lisa. I come from Berlin.
steps.note: Also works for introductions, especially when continuing with more info. 'Ich bin' is more casual than 'Ich heiße'.

steps.title: 🤝 The German handshake
steps.text:
Germans shake hands A LOT:

✅ When meeting someone new
✅ At the start of business meetings
✅ When arriving at a party
✅ Even children are taught to shake hands!

The handshake should be:
💪 Firm (not limp!)
👀 With eye contact
⏱️ Brief but definite

Germans also say their LAST NAME first in formal settings:
'Müller. Thomas Müller.' (like James Bond! 🕴️)

This is very different from many cultures where first names come first.

steps.exampleEn: Nice to meet you! I'm Anna.
steps.note: Short for 'Es freut mich, Sie kennenzulernen' (It pleases me to get to know you). 'Freut mich' is the everyday short form.

steps.exampleEn: Very pleased to meet you, Mrs. Schmidt!
steps.note: Very formal — business settings, meeting important people. 'Sehr' = very, 'erfreut' = pleased.

steps.q: In a formal setting, Germans often introduce with:

steps.note: 'Heißt du' (informal) or 'heißen Sie' (formal) — both correct. The 'ß' can also be written 'ss'.
'Freut mich' is short for the full formal phrase. Works in any register.

steps.title: How Are You? 💬
steps.desc: The most common question in German — and why the answer matters more than you think.

steps.note: Short for 'Wie geht es dir?' (How goes it for you?). THE everyday question. Germans actually ANSWER this honestly — don't just say 'fine'!

steps.example: Guten Tag! Wie geht es Ihnen?
steps.note: 'Ihnen' = to you (formal dative). Use with strangers, elders, in business. Germans expect a real answer, even formally.
The safe standard answer. 'Mir geht's gut' = literally 'To me it goes well'. Adding 'danke' is polite.
'Sehr' = very. 'Und dir?' = And you? (informal). 'Und Ihnen?' = And you? (formal). Always ask back!
Germans are HONEST about how they feel! 'Es geht' = I'm surviving. Don't be surprised if someone actually tells you their problems — that's German directness!

steps.example: Nicht so gut. Ich bin müde.
steps.note: Germans will actually say this! In many cultures 'How are you?' is just a greeting — in Germany, it's a REAL question. Be prepared for honest answers.

steps.title: 🇩🇪 German honesty culture
steps.text:
When a German asks 'Wie geht's?', they actually want to know!

🇺🇸 American: 'How are you?' → 'Fine!' (always)
🇩🇪 German: 'Wie geht's?' → honest answer

Common REAL answers:
• Gut, danke! — Good, thanks!
• Ganz gut — Pretty good
• Es geht — It's okay / surviving
• Nicht so gut — Not great
• Ich bin müde — I'm tired

This isn't negative — it's AUTHENTIC.
Germans value honesty over social performance.

This is related to German Direktheit (directness) — one of the most famous German cultural traits.

steps.q: A German colleague says 'Nicht so gut.' This is:
'Wie geht es Ihnen?' is formal because of:

steps.note: 'Und dir?' (informal) or 'Und Ihnen?' (formal). Always ask back — it's polite!
Formal version with 'Ihnen'. This is how you'd greet a colleague, shopkeeper, or stranger.

steps.title: 🎬 Your first German conversation!
steps.text:
Put it all together:

👤 Du: Guten Tag!
👤 Anna: Hallo! Wie geht's?
👤 Du: Gut, danke! Und dir?
👤 Anna: Auch gut! Ich heiße Anna.
👤 Du: Freut mich! Ich heiße [your name].
👤 Anna: Freut mich auch! Bis bald!
👤 Du: Tschüss! Bis bald!

🎉 You just had a FULL German conversation!

steps.q: In the conversation above, what does 'Freut mich auch' mean?
